summary: This is a guest post by Kevin, a woodworker and engineer, reviewing three favorite workshop tools. He advocates for the Porter Cable variable tooth saw blade for its remarkably smooth cuts on a modest table saw, a Husky portable air compressor for its quiet operation and surprising capability despite small size, and the Dremel High Speed Rotary Saw Kit as the definitively superior Dremel model. The post is a practical, no-nonsense set of recommendations from someone who values getting maximum performance from affordable, compact tools rather than buying the biggest equipment available.
key_points: A quality blade matters more than saw size — a Porter Cable variable tooth blade transformed a modest 10-inch Craftsman table saw's ability to cut oak
The Husky portable compressor proves that sacrificing raw power for portability, quiet operation, and small footprint is a worthwhile trade-off for home workshops
The Dremel High Speed Rotary Saw Kit is strictly superior to the standard model for only ~$10 more, coming with flex shaft, plunge router kit, and more horsepower
money_quotes: When the wood is passed through you almost don't hear it cutting. The wood just disappears, it's almost freaky.
For $35 who cares about longevity, if it wears out, buy a new one.
It's as far from industrial strength as you can get. But that's what's so great.
If you aren't willing to sacrifice shop space, you aren't willing to have your ears ringing and you aren't willing to shell out more than $200 then this is the perfect compressor.
Heck, if you are willing to spend $200 I recommend you buy 2.
key_insight: Smart tool choices that prioritize the right attributes — blade quality over saw size, portability over raw power, value over brand prestige — can deliver outsized results in a home workshop.
